name: Kuwaiti Arabic

ancestors.notes: Gulf Arabic base — inherits *q→[ɡ], velar affrication, interdental retention and emphatic spreading.

notes: Kuwaiti Arabic — the best-documented Gulf variety. Inherits the Gulf features: *q → [ɡ] (with [q] a formal/MSA candidate); velar affrication /k/→[tʃ] and /ɡ/→[dʒ] before high front /i, iː/; interdentals /θ ð ðˤ/ retained. Kuwaiti-specific: Matar (1969), as reported in Alshammari 2026 p.1335, describes /ɡ/ → [dʒ] adjacent to front vowels, sometimes across an intervening /r, l/ (e.g. qariːb→dʒiriːb), and in emphatic contexts (ðˤiːg → ðˤiːdʒ) — the non-adjacent trigger is NOT modelled (see GULF_G_AFFRICATION notes). The 2nd-person-feminine-singular suffix affricates to -tʃ (kashkasha), a morphological process outside base-orthography scope. Heavy English influence (post-1950s oil era): /p/, /v/, /ŋ/ well-established from loanwords. Input contract: fully diacritised (tashkeel) Arabic.

sources.author: Alshammari, K. K.
sources.title: An Optimality-Theoretic Account of Velar Affrication in Northern Najdi Arabic
sources.publisher: Journal of Language Teaching and Research 17(4)
sources.notes: Read in full (open-access PDF). Reports (p.1335) Matar's (1969) study of Kuwaiti /ɡ/→[dʒ] affrication adjacent to front vowels and in emphatic context, and Johnstone (1967) on the lexicalisation of velar affrication in Eastern Arabic.

sources.author: Al-Balushi, R.
sources.title: Omani Arabic: More than a Dialect
sources.notes: Read the phonology sections. Notes /q/→[ɣ] in some Kuwaiti varieties (e.g. ɣirš 'shark' < qirš), cross-referenced with Eastern Arabian /q/ reflexes (p.89).
